scenario: Team uses Camunda 8 SaaS. Needs to manage clusters -- create, configure, monitor. Wonders Console capabilities.
question: Camunda Console cluster management?

options.text: Camunda Console (SaaS) provides cluster management: create clusters by selecting region, plan size (development, production), and Camunda version; configure secrets for Connector credentials; set IP allowlists to restrict API access; monitor cluster health via built-in dashboards showing broker status and resource utilization; manage team members with role-based access (Developer, Analyst, Admin); provision API client credentials (Client ID and Client Secret) for worker applications; and manage subscription plan details. Console is the primary control plane for all SaaS cluster operations.

Camunda manages everything -- this is only partially correct. In Camunda SaaS, Camunda manages the underlying infrastructure (servers, networking, Zeebe broker configuration, automatic upgrades) but customers retain significant control through Console: creating and deleting clusters, configuring secrets, setting IP allowlists, managing user access and roles, provisioning API client credentials, and viewing monitoring dashboards. Customers are not passive; they configure the operational aspects of their clusters through Console. The division is: Camunda manages infrastructure; customers manage configuration and operational settings.

Console exists precisely to give customers control over their Camunda SaaS clusters. Customers can create new clusters, choose regions and plan sizes, configure secrets for Connector credentials, set IP allowlists to restrict API access, manage team member access with role-based assignments, provision and revoke API client credentials, view cluster health metrics, and configure backup and maintenance window settings. This level of self-service control is a core value proposition of the SaaS offering.

API only -- this is only partially correct. While Camunda does provide a Console API for programmatic cluster management (enabling infrastructure-as-code and CI/CD integration), the Console web UI is the primary interface for most teams. The UI provides visual dashboards, point-and-click cluster creation, secrets management forms, user invitation workflows, and metric visualizations. Teams not using Terraform or automation scripts interact with Console through the UI. The API complements the UI for automation scenarios rather than replacing it.

a.text: Correct. Console capabilities: Cluster lifecycle: Create cluster: Choose region (e.g., EU, US). Choose plan / size. Choose Camunda version. Configure name. Configure cluster: Secrets management (per Set 16 Q33). IP allowlist: restrict access. Backup / restore (per Set 14 Q55). Maintenance windows: scheduled updates. Monitor cluster: Health dashboard: green / yellow / red. Metrics: throughput, latency. Component status: Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist. Delete cluster: With confirmation: destructive. Backup before: optional. Access management: User invitations: Invite by email. Assign role. Organization-level roles per console/manage-organization/manage-users.md:11-30: Owner: all rights; exactly one per org per :11 ("An organization cannot have more than one owner"). Admin: full access to platform/processes/clusters, but cannot manage other admins per :17-19. Modeler: Web Modeler access (no deploy/run); read-only Console per :23. Analyst: Modeler perms + full Optimize per :24. Per-cluster authorizations (new in 8.8) per :26-28: Cluster-level access is configured independently in Admin (Orchestration Cluster), not via org roles. "Developer" / "Viewer" labels are not documented org roles. Users can hold multiple roles (e.g., Modeler + Analyst) per :30. API access: Client credentials: Create OAuth client: per integration. Scopes: limit access. Revoke: when no longer needed. Subscription management: Plan upgrade / downgrade. Usage tracking. Billing. Audit log: Operations log: who did what. Compliance: traceability. Multi-region: Clusters in different regions: per Set 14 Q56. Data residency: per region. Latency optimisation: regional. For Self-Managed: No Console typically: deploy + manage manually. Web Modeler may be available (verify). Custom dashboards: Grafana, etc. Operations console vs management console: Camunda Console (SaaS): subscription + cluster management. Web Modeler: BPMN modeling. Operate: instance monitoring. Tasklist: User Task UI. Different audiences: Console: ops admins, business owners. Web Modeler: BPMN authors. Operate: operators, developers. Tasklist: end users. For SaaS billing: Per cluster cost: based on plan + usage. Visible in Console. For SaaS support: Support tickets: via Console.
